- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.

- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>final_class</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

First list the critical observable features present only in the Query Image.
Then formulate a knowledge base (kb) with a small set of IF-THEN rules grounded in observable patterns from the labeled examples in the support images and the Query Image, for all classes. Try to unify the conditions so that there is only one rule per class.
Rules must refer only to visible features.
Then check which rule(s) are best satisfied by the Query Image.

- observable feature: feature value

</kb>

referring only to the features listed above. Do not discuss other rules or introduce new features.
</rule_check>

<response>final_class</response>

raw_response_text:
<features>
- coat color: black and tan
- ears: erect, pointed
- body size: small
- fur length: short
</features>
<kb>
- IF [coat color is black and tan OR coat color is tan/light brown AND ears are erect and pointed AND body size is small AND fur length is short] THEN [21]
- IF [coat color is white or light-colored AND fur is long and fluffy AND body size is large] THEN [15]
- IF [coat color is white, grey, or brown with colorpoint markings OR bi-color AND fur is long and fluffy AND eyes are blue or dark] THEN [26]
</kb>
<rule_check>
- The rule for class 21 fires because the Query Image shows a small dog with a black and tan coat, short fur, and erect, pointed ears, which matches the observable features for this class.
</rule_check>
<response>21</response>

original_messages.content:
# TASK
1. Analyze the provided labeled examples to identify the observable visual features that distinguish each class.
2. Examine the Query Image and compare it strictly against the provided examples.
3. Determine which label from the provided options best matches the Query Image.

# INSTRUCTIONS
- Base your decision only on observable visual evidence in the examples and the Query Image.
- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags and not any aditional ones.
- Do not output any text outside the XML tags.
- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>final_class</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

First list the critical observable features present only in the Query Image.
Then formulate a knowledge base (kb) with a small set of IF-THEN rules grounded in observable patterns from the labeled examples in the support images and the Query Image, for all classes. Try to unify the conditions so that there is only one rule per class.
Rules must refer only to visible features.
Then check which rule(s) are best satisfied by the Query Image.

<features>
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
</features>
<kb>
- IF [observable visual features = feature value AND/OR observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class1]
- IF [observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class2]
</kb>

<rule_check>
- State which rule(s) from the kb fires for the Query Image and briefly explain why,
referring only to the features listed above. Do not discuss other rules or introduce new features.
</rule_check>

<response>final_class</response>

The content of <response> must be exactly one label copied verbatim from the provided options list.

raw_response_text:
<features>
- animal type: cat
- fur color: predominantly white with some light brown/grey on tail and ears
- fur length: medium to long
- eye color: blue
- ears: pointed, upright
- body size: small (kitten)
</features>
<kb>
- IF [animal type = dog AND fur color = black and tan OR fur color = tan OR fur color = cream AND ears = upright] THEN [21]
- IF [animal type = cat AND fur color = predominantly white/light with some darker markings AND fur length = medium to long AND eye color = blue OR dark] THEN [26]
- IF [animal type = dog AND fur color = white/cream AND fur length = long AND body size = large] THEN [15]
</kb>
<rule_check>
- The rule for class 26 fires because the Query Image shows an animal that is a cat, with predominantly white/light fur and some darker markings, medium to long fur length, and blue eyes.
</rule_check>
<response>26</response>

original_messages.content:
# TASK
1. Analyze the provided labeled examples to identify the observable visual features that distinguish each class.
2. Examine the Query Image and compare it strictly against the provided examples.
3. Determine which label from the provided options best matches the Query Image.

# INSTRUCTIONS
- Base your decision only on observable visual evidence in the examples and the Query Image.
- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags and not any aditional ones.
- Do not output any text outside the XML tags.
- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>final_class</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

First list the critical observable features present only in the Query Image.
Then formulate a knowledge base (kb) with a small set of IF-THEN rules grounded in observable patterns from the labeled examples in the support images and the Query Image, for all classes. Try to unify the conditions so that there is only one rule per class.
Rules must refer only to visible features.
Then check which rule(s) are best satisfied by the Query Image.

<features>
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
</features>
<kb>
- IF [observable visual features = feature value AND/OR observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class1]
- IF [observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class2]
</kb>

<rule_check>
- State which rule(s) from the kb fires for the Query Image and briefly explain why,
referring only to the features listed above. Do not discuss other rules or introduce new features.
</rule_check>

<response>final_class</response>

The content of <response> must be exactly one label copied verbatim from the provided options list.

raw_response_text:
<features>
- coat color: white/cream
- coat texture: long, thick, fluffy
- body size: large
- ears: medium-sized, floppy
- facial expression: mouth open, tongue visible
</features>
<kb>
- IF [coat color is white/cream AND coat texture is long, thick, fluffy AND body size is large] THEN [15]
- IF [coat color is black and tan OR tan AND body size is small AND ears are erect or semi-erect] THEN [21]
- IF [coat color is white/cream with colorpoint markings OR grey/white OR brown/white AND coat texture is long/medium AND eyes are blue] THEN [26]
</kb>
<rule_check>
- The rule for class 15 fires because the Query Image shows a dog with a white/cream, long, thick, fluffy coat and a large body size.
</rule_check>
<response>15</response>
